title_tesim: Bedroom in the Arroyo Seco house of Olive Percival, Los Angeles, 1900-1944

description_tesim: Photograph of a bedroom in the house of Olive Percival. The view is from the bed, with a turned post footboard, towards a wall with a dresser and slant front writing desk, and with two framed silhouettes and a portrait on the wall above. A bookcase is on the right.
Olive Percival's home, Down-hyl Claim, was located in the Arroyo Seco area of north-east Los Angeles, at 522 San Pascual Avenue.

description_tesim: Photograph of the living room in the house of Olive Percival with a stone-faced fireplace with wood plank mantle and sofa (left), and bookcases in the corner beyond. In addition to books, the bookcase holds candlesticks and vases. There is an oriental carpet on the floor.
Olive Percival's home, Down-hyl Claim, was located in the Arroyo Seco area of north-east Los Angeles, at 522 San Pascual Avenue.

title_tesim: Olive Percival's Arroyo Seco house, with her cat perched in front, Los Angeles, 1912
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative: 4-11-12
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Olive Percival Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Oliver Percival's house and garden in the Arroyo Seco area of northeast Los Angeles. The house is a two-story, half-timber construction. A cat is perched on a sundial in the abundant garden with flowers, a century plant, a vine growing on the house and trees. A wind mill for the well is visible beside a tall, conical tree.
Olive Percival's home, Down-hyl Claim, was located in the Arroyo Seco area of north-east Los Angeles, at 522 San Pascual Avenue.

Round wooden cheese boxes, painted by Olive Percival to re-purpose as hatboxes, arranged on a patio or terrace at her home, Down-hyl Claim. Painted images include a peacock, trees, hearts, and female figures; one box has "Olive" decoratively rendered and 2 others have her initials "O.P."

title_tesim: Hildegarde Flanner at the entrance to the cemetery at Mission San Luis Rey, Oceanside, 1927
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Hildegarde Flanner 7/27; Charles Francis Saunders 6/27
Handwritten on negative: 10-12-27
named_subject_tesim: Mission San Luis Rey de Francia (Oceanside, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Photograph of Hildegarde Flanner at the entrance to the cemetery at Mission San Luis Rey. A skull and cross bones are represented in relief on the wall above the doorway. Construction supplies and equipment are on the ground including lumber and bricks (left) and a wheel barrow (right).

June Hildegarde Flanner Monhoff was an American poet, essayist, playwright and conversationalist who lived in Pasadena, California. Her writings were published in The Nation, The New Republic and Poetry. She was also an avid gardener with a particular interest in grasses and bamboo.
combined_subject_ssim: Mission San Luis Rey de Francia (Oceanside, Calif.)

title_tesim: Royal Presidio Chapel, Monterey, 1900-1930
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Olive Percival Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Also known as the Cathedral of San Carlos Borromeo, the Royal Presidio Chapel is a Roman Catholic cathedral built in 1794. It is the oldest continuously operating parish and the oldest stone building in California. It is the only existing presidio chapel in California and the only existing building in the original Monterey Presidio. The church was founded by the Franciscan Father Junípero Serra as the chapel of Mission San Carlos Borromeo de Carmelo on June 3, 1770. Father Serra first established the original mission in Monterey at this location on June 3, 1770, near the native village of Tamo. However, Father Serra became engaged in a power struggle with Military Governor Pedro Fages, who was headquartered at the Presidio of Monterey. Serra relocated the mission to its current location in the present-day town of Carmel. The new mission was named Mission San Carlos Borromeo de Carmelo.
Exterior view of the front of the Cathedral

description_tesim: The church of the Angeles, at 1100 North Avenue 64, is the oldest church along the Arroyo Seco. It was built by Francis Campbell-Johnson as a memorial to her husband, Alexander, and a place of worship for the people of Garvanza. The church is faced in sandstone that was hauled from quarries in the San Fernando valley. The interior walls of the church are of red pressed brick, and the ceiling is of redwood.

Olive Percival's home, Down-hyl Claim, was located in the Arroyo Seco area of north-east Los Angeles, at 522 San Pascual Avenue.

description_tesim: June Hildegarde Flanner Monhoff was an American poet, essayist, playwright and conversationalist who lived in Pasadena, California. Her writings were published in The Nation, The New Republic and Poetry. She was also an avid gardener with a particular interest in grasses and bamboo.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Interiors & Furniture
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Olive Percival Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the living room in the house of Olive Percival. The view is from a table towards the stone-faced fire place with wood plank mantle. The table holds an oil lamp, books and a small plant arrangement in a bowl. The mantle holds framed images and a flower arrangement  and a Buddha statue sits on a wooden ledge above. There are bookcases along the back wall on either side of the fireplace.
Olive Percival's home, Down-hyl Claim, was located in the Arroyo Seco area of north-east Los Angeles, at 522 San Pascual Avenue.

Photograph of Oliver Percival's house and garden in the Arroyo Seco area of northeast Los Angeles. The house is a half-timber construction fronted by a concrete porch and low poured concrete wall. A stone-lined path leads to the steps and the garden foliage, with includes potted plants on the wall, is abundant. An unrolled bamboo shade covers one window.

title_tesim: Reynolds residence patio garden, Palos Verdes Estates, 1927
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Myra Reynolds' Home, Palos Verdes (Films) taken 15 Oct., 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Olive Percival Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph taken by Olive Percival of Myra Reynolds in the patio garden of her home in Palos Verdes Estates. Dr. Myra Reynolds was a graduate of Vassar, the first fellow to graduate from the University of Chicago, one of the first 3 to graduate from the same institution with a doctorate of philosophy, and was a professor of English literature at the same institution until the age of 70, when she moved to California.

Myra and Emily Reynolds were the original owners of this house located at 1501 Via Montemar, Palos Verdes Estates (Malaga Cove). Built in 1925, it is a two-story, seven-room stucco house with a tile roof, enclosed patio and attached garage, and it overlooks Bluff Cove. The architect was Paul R. Williams and the builder was C.E. McFadyen.

Paul Revere Williams, FAIA was an American architect based in Los Angeles, California. He practiced largely in Southern California and designed the homes of numerous celebrities, including Frank Sinatra, Lucille Ball and Desi Arnaz, Lon Chaney, and Charles Correll. He also designed many public and private buildings. Williams became the first African-American member of the American Institute of Architects (AIA) in 1923.

description_tesim: The Old Corner Book Store was built in 1712 and is said to be the oldest brick building in Boston. It is located at the corner of Washington and School Streets.

View of the Old Corner Bookstore Building, photographed by bibliophile Olive Percival during a trip to Boston, with well-dressed men and women and a horse and carriage going about their business on the brick-paved street in front. Tenant signs read: "Pants to measure in any style $3.50. Eclipse Pants Co. No.3," "Most Liberal Loans at Lowest Interests," and "Fresh Home Made."

title_tesim: Exterior view of the Chee Kung Tong Co. building on Marchessault Street in old Chinatown, Los Angeles, 1920-33
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Chinatown 11/25/11
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Olive Percival Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The 1911 date is probably incorrect because another image of this building in this collection, also dated to 1911 on the negative sleeve, has striped awnings and in this image the striped awings have worn away.

Photograph of a 2-story brick building on Marchessault Street in old Chinatown in Los Angeles with 2 automobiles parked on the unpaved street in front and 3 men standing in front and 2 other walking by. A sign at the center top of the facade reads "Chee Kung Tong Co." The building has three wooden-rail balconies and brick work and relief ornament on the upper story.
Marchessault Street was in old Chinatown, in the vicinity of Alameda and Aliso Streets and it disappeared during the construction of Union Station.
Old Chinatown was demolished starting in 1933 to make way for Union Station.

description_tesim: Rebecca Buffum Spring and her husband, Marcus Spring, were intensely involved in liberal political and social affairs and were part of the abolitionist, feminist, and transcendentalist movements.  In the late 1890s, she moved to southern California to live with her daughter, Jeanie Peet, where she became involved with many of the local artists and writers.

description_tesim: Photograph taken by Olive Percival of the Lily pond amid flower beds in the patio of the home of Myra and Emily Reynolds in Palos Verdes Estates

Myra and Emily Reynolds were the original owners of this house located at 1501 Via Montemar, Palos Verdes Estates (Malaga Cove). Built in 1925, it is a two-story, seven-room stucco house with a tile roof, enclosed patio and attached garage, and it overlooks Bluff Cove. The architect was Paul R. Williams and the builder was C.E. McFadyen.

description_tesim: A view of the bell cote from the back at San Antonio de Pala Asistencia, showing steps leading up to the bell cote. The bell cote (bell gable, campanario, espadaña) is unique among the California missions as a free-standing structure. The original bell cote was destroyed by torrential rains in 1916 and was replaced by a precise replica immediately.  The Pala Mission was a sub-mission to Mission San Luis Rey de Francia, some twenty miles inland upstream from the mission on the San Luis Rey River.
combined_subject_ssim: San Antonio de Pala Asistencia (Pala, Calif.)

description_tesim: Cameraman Frank Goodliffe and Margaret Rotha, wife of filmmaker Paul Rotha, on the Acropolis with the Propylaea in the background and a Parvo ("Debrie") motion picture camera on a tripod in front of them. They are smoking cigarettes and a bottle of wine, two wine glasses and a paper bag are on a large pavement stone next to Margaret's feet. On a block of stone on the left is a Newman-Sinclair motion picture camera.

"Contact" (1933) is a documentary film by Paul Rotha. Made for Imperial Airways and sponsored by Shell-Mex and BP Ltd, "Contact" shows the construction of an airplane and how air travel is opening up the world. It was filmed in England and along air routes in the Middle East, Africa and Athens.

Five men and two fuel tanks on wheels in a desert, perhaps at a runway during the filming of Contact. The two on the left wear pullovers with "Shell" printed on the front and trousers, with one wearing a turban and shoes and the other a cap and no shoes. The center man wears pullover with "Imperial Airways" printed on the front, short trousers and a turban. The fourth man wears a pullover, trousers, cap and no shoes. The man on the right wears a suit and fez and is probably a Shell employee.

description_tesim: Date from Rotha, Paul, A Paul Rotha Reader (1999), p. 261

The DH.66 Hercules was a seven-passenger, three-engined airliner built by De Havilland Aircraft Company. The Imperial Airways plane "City of Karachi" flew the last segment of the central Africa route, initiated in 1931, to Cape Town.

Cameraman Horace Wheddon with three local men who are carrying who pieces of luggage and a tripod on a road after the forced landing of the "City of Karachi" transport plane (a De Havilland DH.66 Hercules) in Kigwe (near Dodoma, Tanzania). The plane, two small buildings and other people are in the background, with a hill visible beyond.

description_tesim: “Contact" (1933) is a documentary film by Paul Rotha. View of a Ugandan tribal chief seated in the open entrance of a traditional round dwelling of grass-thatched wattle and daub on a raised chair covered by a large leopard skin which covers the ground across the opening. A long elephant tusk on the ground in front of  the leader. He is wearing a fez hat and a full length embroidered gown and holding up a fly wisk as a symbol of authority and he is flanked by a standing guard with a tall spear and three drummers, one with an engalabi drum and the others with engoma (ngoma) drums. Photograph taken on a journey of filmmaker Paul Rotha and his party to Murchison Falls during the filming of "Contact."

"Contact" (1933) is a documentary film by Paul Rotha. Made for Imperial Airways and sponsored by Shell-Mex and BP Ltd, "Contact" shows the construction of an airplane and how air travel is opening up the world. It was filmed in England and along air routes in the Middle East, Africa and Athens.

“Contact" (1933) is a documentary film by Paul Rotha. Made for Imperial Airways and sponsored by Shell-Mex and BP Ltd, "Contact" shows the construction of an airplane and how air travel was opening up the world. It was filmed in England and along air routes in the Middle East, Africa and Athens. In the chapter "Making Contact 1932-33 (1973)," in A Paul Rotha Reader, Rotha writes about riding camels in Aswān in January 1933.

The DH.66 Hercules was a seven-passenger, three-engined airliner built by De Havilland Aircraft Company. The Imperial Airways plane "City of Karachi" flew the last segment of the central Africa route, initiated in 1931,  to Cape Town.

View of the underside of the wings of the transport plane, "City of Karachi" (a De Havilland DH.66 Hercules), with tangled thorn bushes, after a forced landing in Kigwe (near Dodoma, Tanzania) during the filming of "Contact."

"Contact" (1933) is a documentary film by Paul Rotha. Made for Imperial Airways and sponsored by Shell-Mex and BP Ltd, "Contact" shows the construction of an airplane and how air travel was opening up the world. It was filmed in England and along air routes in the Middle East, Africa and Athens.

Filmmaker Paul Rotha stands next to him. On a block of stone on the left is a Newman-Sinclair motion picture camera. On the ground behind Goodliffe is a bottle of wine, two wine glasses and a paper bag.

title_tesim: Jeanne Crain, Mamie Van Doren, Kitty Kallen and others on set in The Second Greatest Sex
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: 1794-P43, The Second Greatest Sex, Universal-International; Strange Inhabitants -- Universal-International's famous Western Street is populated by women only for the first time in its spectacular screen career as it serves as a setting for U-I's "The Second Greatest Sex." Here, the CinemaScope camera moves in on a sequence involving Kitty Kallen, Jeanne Crain, Mamie Van Doren and others. Filmed in both CinemaScope and Technicolor, the rollicking musical of what "Lysistrata" did to a small Kansas town features an all-star cast headed by Miss Crain, George Nader, Miss Kallen, Bert Lahr, Keith Andes, Paul Gilbert, Miss Van Doren and Tommy Rall.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Danny Kaye and Virginia Mayo in a scene from The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
uniform_title_tesim: Secret Life Of Walter Mitty, The  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: SG-2000 -- 260, 263, 265, 267, 274, 278; THROUGH STORM AND GALE -- Dauntless Walter Mitty (Danny Kaye) grasps the helm with confidence, and his sweetheart Virginia Mayo holds on with utmost faith in her hero. It seems as if Mitty is having another of his famous daydreams, and forgets that he is only an employee of a fantastic fiction magazine in Samuel Goldwyn's Technicolor comedy, "The Secret Life of Walter Mitty", directed by Norman Z. McLeod, co-starring Mr. Kaye and Miss Mayo, with Boris Karloff, Fay Bainter and Ann Rutherford, and based on the James Thurber story.

title_tesim: Walter Brennan and Gary Cooper in Sergeant York
caption_tesim: Stamped in black ink text from back of photograph:UCLA Theatre Arts Library
Handwritten in pencil text from back of photograph: Bob Thomas Coll.
Handwritten in pencil text from back of photograph: Negative Available ASC 843
Stamped and handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph:Theater Arts LibraryMPS 948Sergeant York.
Stamped in pink ink text from back of photograph: APR 22 1941Call-Bulletin Library
Handwritten in blue ink text from back of photograph: Sergeant York

Pasted-on paper with typed text from back of photograph: When Warner Bros. cast Gary Cooper in the starring role in SERGEANT YORK, the studio astutely placed Walter Brennan in the role of Pastor Pile, who preaches pacifism to him. Brennan has always been a perfect foil for the screen acting of Cooper. He ably supported the lanky actor in "Meet John Doe" and "The Westerner," for which he won an Academy Award. Another interesting bit of casting was giving Joan Leslie, sensational young newcomer, the role of Alvin C. York's sweetheart in the film, which is directed by Howard Hawks.
Stamped in black ink text from back of photograph: Mac Julian  Warner Bros.License to reproduce with copyright notice granted newspapers, magazines and other periodicals.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Jennifer Jones and Marshall Thompson on the set of Good Morning, Miss Dove
caption_tesim: Handwritten in pencil text from back of photograph: Jenny JonesRobt Hutton [sic]in Good Morning Miss Dove[actor Robert Hutton does not appear in the film "Good Morning, Miss Dove"]
Handwritten in purple ink text from back of photograph: 1
Stamped and handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph:Theater Arts LibraryMPS-340-52X
Stamped in black ink text from back of photograph: UCLA Theater Arts Library
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Difficult to identify photograph of actress Jennifer Jones and (possibly) actor Marshall Thompson, apparently on the set of the 1955 film, "Good Morning Miss Dove." A few discrepancies emerge: 1.) The handwritten pencil text on the back of photo identifies the name of the film as such, but it lists the actor in the photo as 'Robt Hutton.' Actor Robert Hutton does not appear in this film, nor in any film with actress Jennifer Jones. 2.) The only character and scene in the film that represents any romantic interest from Jennifer Jones' 'Miss Dove' character, is one scene with actor Marshall Thompson, who portrays her love interest, 'Wilfred Pendleton.' However, both actors are wearing completely different outfits in the film's scene. 3.) The photo depicts an outdoor scenario and the scene in the film is indoors. 4.) The actor in the photograph bears a similar resemblance to actor Marshall Thompson, but positive identification is difficult. 5.) Perhaps this photograph is taken from a scene that did not make the final cut of the film or was simply an early promotional still. 6.) The number etched into the lower right corner of the image is 925-52x, and the 925 prefix does match another photo correctly representing this film in the collection (Ark Id#: 21198/zz001nt3z8), as its etched number is 925-74. 7.) Ignoring that connection, the only other possible explanation, is that this photograph is taken from a scene from an entirely different movie with an entirely different actor, yet to be identified.
